summary: McKenzie documents his early micro-ISV expenses, totaling just $32.22 to launch a business, and describes a marketing insight about selling Fourth of July bingo cards on eBay. He identifies that millions of consumers will search for holiday activities in a compressed three-day window, creating a traffic spike he couldn't afford to buy through advertising. He plans to price the product at $9.99 to capture impulse purchases, calculating roughly $7 profit per sale after fees. The post captures the scrappy, analytical mindset of a bootstrapped founder calculating every dollar while hunting for asymmetric marketing opportunities.
key_points: Total business launch costs were just $32.22, split between a fax, Google AdWords deposit, and domain registration — illustrating how cheaply a micro-ISV could start in 2006
Seasonal search spikes around holidays create predictable surges in demand that can be exploited with targeted products
eBay's existing market of home consumers buying bingo cards at $8+$5 shipping validates demand while representing a different segment than his core teacher market
The downside risk is trivial (the cost of five AdWords clicks) while the upside could make the business cash-flow positive on day two
money_quotes: 2000 yen = $17.20! Thats almost a third of my budget, and while I'll make it up on my first transaction it was rather irksome.
This brings my total expenditure to date to $32.22.
If you check the search engine results, you get crazy spikes around all major holidays for exactly those seasonal bingo games — strange but true!
Short time frame for one-use impulse-purchase Americana… I SMELL EBAY!
Millions of them are going to be searching for Fourth of July activities over a three day period. I can't even buy that kind of traffic.
If it turns out to have been a stupid idea, oh well, I'm out the price of about five clicks from Google AdWords.
And wouldn't that be a funny thing to announce on day two of owning your own business.
key_insight: The best early-stage marketing bets have trivial downside and asymmetric upside — a seasonal product targeting a predictable demand spike costs almost nothing to test but could make the business profitable immediately.
